content.full_text:
JAMES V. JONES
LAW OFFICES
1700 SECOND STREET
NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94558
(707) 252-8644

January 19, 1977

Mr. Michael J. Baker
State of California
Fair Political Practice Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Baker:

Thank you for your letter of January 3, 1977. I believe that I
can understand the Commission's position with respect to it's on
going responsibilities to enforce the law as it exists on the
books.

Based upon my strong concern for the underlying attorney-client
principal/privilege, and in view of the fact that I am no longer
holding Public Office, I believe that I must in conscience main-
tain my present position with regard to schedule 721-P pending
the outcome of Hayes V. Wood. If you feel that enforcement pro-
ceedings should be presently effected in my case, would you please
let me know.

Thank you for your continuing cooperation and courtesy.

Very truly yours,

JAMES V. JONES

JVJ:smc

January 3, 1977


James W. Jones
1720 Second St.
Napa, CA 94558

Re: Statement of Economic
Interests

Dear Mr. Jones:

Michael Gennett has asked that I respond to your
letter of December 6, 1976, regarding completion of Schedule
721-J of your Statement of Economic Interests.

We understand your desire that Raya V. Wood at
finally resolved before you decide whether to comply with the
client disclosure requirement of the Political Reform Act.
You must also understand, however, our responsibility to
apply the law as written until a court directs us otherwise.
In that connection, you should be aware of an enclosed letter
sent at the request of Robert Sweet of the State Bar outlining
our enforcement policy regarding the client disclosure requirement of the Act pending final determination of Davis v.
Wood.

If you have any questions, please do not hesitate
to call me.

Sincerely yours,


Michael J. Baker
Staff Counsel

MJB/rjh
Enclosure

bcc: Jeanne, Mickey, Pawn

JAMES V. JONES
LAW OFFICES
1700 SECOND STREET
NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94558

(707) 252-8644

December 8, 1976


Mr. Michael Bennett
State of California
Fair Political Practice Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Bennett:

Thank you for your letter of November 1, 1976, inquiring
into the manner in which I completed schedule 721-F.

I am sorry for the delay in responding to your letter, which
was occasioned by my researching the cases cited in the order
adopting regulations of the commission which you kindly
provided in your letter.

My research led me to discuss the matter somewhat with the
State Bar, and also with Mr. Hays, City Attorney for the
City of Ukiah, California. Since it appears that the very
question involved in my not listing my clients on schedule
721-F, is now on appeal before the State Appellate Court
(Hays v. Wood), I am hereby respectfully requesting that
the matter of my completion of this schedule be suspended
pending the outcome of that case.

I intend to comply with the law, but I am so concerned about
this regulation as an erosion of the attorney client privilege
that I believe awaiting a statement by the appellate court
directly on this point is a necessity.

If you disagree with my request, would you please let me know.

Thank you for your attention.

Very truly yours,

James V. Jones,

JVJ/sh

July 13, 1976


Robert M. Sweet, Esq.
The State Bar of California
1230 West Third Street
Los Angeles, CA 90017

Dear Bob:

On July 7, 1976, the Commission met in executive session
to discuss the matter you raised with me by telephone on June 30,
1976, respecting the Commission's policy regarding enforcement of
Government Code Section 87207. During the executive session the
Commission affirmed the policy that I described to you in our
telephone conversation of July 1, 1976.

To reiterate, the Commission will continue to enforce
the law as written until such time as the law is stayed by a court
order, ruled invalid by a court or changed by the Legislature.
Accordingly, we shall inform persons who decline to comply with
Section 87207(b) that they are required to do so and that noncompliance places them in violation of the Political Reform Act.

As a practical matter, we regard Hays v. Wood as a test
case with respect to the constitutionality of Section 87207(b)(2)
and, therefore, have not initiated any other judicial
or administrative enforcement proceedings pending the outcome of
that lawsuit. However, this practice should not be considered
tantamount to a policy of nonenforcement. We still expect public
officials with disclosure obligations under the Act to comply
fully. Further, the Commission reserves the right to initiate
other litigation or administrative enforcement proceedings should
it be deemed appropriate in a particular situation.

You raised this issue in the context of a letter that
had come to your attention, which was written by Del Spurlock,
Chief of our Conflict of Interest Division, to Richard Will, General
Counsel of the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California.
In order to clarify the position of the Commission, Del is drafting
a second letter to Mr. Will outlining the points I have set forth
above.

I appreciate your calling me about this matter. If I
can provide any further clarification, please let me know.

Very truly yours,


Michael J. Baker
Staff Counsel

sections.question_synthetic: The document consists of correspondence from attorney James V. Jones to the FPPC regarding his completion of Schedule 721-F and his concerns about attorney-client privilege in disclosing client information.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is incoming correspondence from a private citizen/attorney to the FPPC, not an FPPC advice letter or response. No formal Question or Conclusion sections present. Document appears to be OCR'd with some character recognition errors ('it's' for 'its', '&' rendering). Two related letters included in single document, both requesting case suspension pending appellate court decision.

embedding.summary: These are two outgoing letters from James V. Jones to the FPPC (dated December 8, 1976 and January 19, 1977) requesting suspension of enforcement regarding Schedule 721-F completion pending the outcome of Hayes v. Wood appellate case, citing attorney-client privilege concerns.
